Examples of acceptable documents: - Emergency Action Plan (United States, Canada, Mexico) - Plan d'Évacuation d'Urgence (European Union countries) - Emergency Response Procedures (Australia, Singapore) - Plano de Emergência (Brazil) - Plan de Emergencia (Argentina) - 緊急時対応計画 (Japan) - خطة الطوارئ (Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, Egypt, UAE) - Acil Durum Planı (Turkey) - आपातकालीन कार्य योजना (India) Evaluation criteria: - Procedures for reporting a fire or other emergency - Procedures for emergency evacuation, including type of evacuation and exit route assignments - Procedures to be followed by employees who remain to operate critical plant operations before they evacuate - Procedures to account for all employees after evacuation - Procedures to be followed by employees performing rescue or medical duties - The name or job title of every employee who may be contacted by employees who need more information about the plan or an explanation of their duties under the plan.
Upload your organization's Emergency Action Plan or equivalent emergency response document. This document should outline basic emergency procedures and evacuation routes.

Examples of acceptable documents: - Emergency Response Training Program outline or curriculum - Training matrices showing emergency response training requirements by role - Emergency Response Team (ERT) training schedules - Training attendance sheets for emergency drills - Competency assessments for emergency responders Evaluation criteria: - Multiple scenario coverage: Training must address different emergency scenarios (fire, medical, severe weather, chemical spill, security threat, etc.) - Role-specific training: Different training requirements defined for general employees, emergency response team members, floor wardens, and incident commanders - Training frequency: Clearly defined initial and refresher training intervals (initial training for new employees within 30 days, annual refreshers minimum) - Learning objectives: Specific, measurable learning objectives for each training module including knowledge and skill demonstrations - Practical exercises: Evidence of hands-on training components including fire extinguisher use, evacuation drills, first aid practice, and spill response - Competency verification: Methods for assessing trainee understanding through tests, demonstrations, or drill performance evaluations - Training materials: Comprehensive training materials including presentations, videos, handouts, and job aids - Instructor qualifications: Defined qualifications for emergency response trainers including certifications and experience requirements
Upload documentation showing your comprehensive training program for emergency preparedness.

Examples of acceptable documents: - Signed attendance sheets from emergency response drill - Certificates of completion for emergency training courses - Drill participation records - Emergency response team member certifications Evaluation criteria: - Participation rate: Evidence that at least 90% of employees have completed required emergency training within the past 12 months - Training completion tracking: Individual training records showing dates, topics covered, and trainer information - Drill participation: Records showing regular participation in emergency drills (minimum quarterly for fire drills) - Specialized training: Evidence of advanced training for emergency response team members (first aid, firefighting, hazmat response) - New employee training: Documentation showing new employees receive emergency training during orientation - Visitor/contractor briefing: Records of emergency procedure briefings for visitors and contractors - Training effectiveness: Evidence of post-training assessments or drill performance improvements over time
Upload records demonstrating worker participation in emergency preparedness training.

Examples of acceptable documents: - Joint exercise plans with fire departments, police, Emergency Medical Services (EMS) - Multi-organization drill scenarios - Mutual aid exercise documentation - Community emergency response exercises - Industrial park or business district joint drill records - Government emergency management agency exercise participation records Evaluation criteria: - External participation scope: Exercises must include at least 1 external agencies (fire, police, emergency medical services, emergency management) - Exercise complexity: Multi-agency scenarios testing interoperability, unified command, and resource coordination - Communication testing: Evidence of testing interoperable communications between organizations including radio systems and command posts - Unified command structure: Documentation of Incident Command System (ICS) implementation with clear integration of external agencies - Resource coordination: Testing of resource requests, mutual aid activation, and equipment/personnel sharing - Joint planning process: Evidence of collaborative exercise design with input from all participating agencies - Scenario realism: Exercises based on credible threats requiring genuine multi-agency response - Frequency of joint exercises: Minimum of one major multi-agency exercise annually plus smaller coordination drills - Geographic considerations: Exercises address access routes, staging areas, and traffic control with local authorities - Mass casualty elements: If applicable, exercises include triage, transport, and hospital coordination components
Upload documentation showing exercises conducted with external stakeholders and first responders.

Examples of acceptable documents: - Worker Participation Policy - Risk Assessment Procedure (Procedimiento de Evaluación de Riesgos) - Hazard Identification Program (Programme d'Identification des Dangers) - Safety Management System (SMS) Manual sections on worker involvement - Contractor Safety Participation Requirements (متطلبات مشاركة المقاول في السلامة) - Joint Health and Safety Committee (JHSC) Terms of Reference - Worker Consultation Procedure (कर्मचारी परामर्श प्रक्रिया) - Participative Risk Assessment Protocol (協議型リスクアセスメント手順) - Health and Safety Program with worker participation sections (Programa de Saúde e Segurança) Detailed Evaluation criteria: 1\. Mandatory participation language: Document must contain clear requirements (using words like "shall," "must," "will," or "required") for input from all three groups 2\. Scope coverage: Policy must explicitly name management, workers, AND contractors as required participants 3\. Input mechanisms: Document should specify how input is gathered (such as meetings, surveys, observations, or reviews) 4\. Timing requirements: Policy should indicate when participation occurs (such as initial assessment, periodic reviews, or after incidents) 5\. Responsibility assignments: Clear identification of who is responsible for ensuring participation occurs
Upload your organization's policy, program, or procedure that mandates management, workers, and contractors to provide input during hazard identification and risk assessment activities.

Examples of acceptable documents for training: - Training attendance sheets (Hojas de Asistencia a Capacitación) - Learning Management System (LMS) completion reports - Training certificates for hazard identification (Zertifikate für Gefährdungsbeurteilung) - Toolbox talk records on risk assessment participation (Registros de Charlas de Seguridad) - Competency assessments for risk evaluation (تقييمات الكفاءة لتقييم المخاطر) - Workshop attendance records (ワークショップ参加記録) - Training matrices showing completion status (Matriz de Treinamento) Detailed Evaluation criteria: - Names and roles of attendees clearly documented - Training content specifically addresses risk assessment participation - Evidence of attendance from all three groups (management, workers, contractors) - Date of training is visible and current (typically within last 12-24 months) - Training objectives or outline shows coverage of input methods
Upload training records showing management, workers, and contractors have been trained on providing input to risk assessments

Examples of completed risk assessments with participant documentation: - Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment Documents with reviewer signatures from multiple levels - Risk registers showing multi-level review (Registros de Riesgos con Revisión Multinivel) - Permit to Work (PTW) with risk assessment sections showing diverse input - Hazard and Operability Study (HAZOP) reports with participant lists - What-If Analysis documentation with team composition - Safety walkthrough reports with participant roster (安全巡視報告書) - Contractor pre-job risk assessments (تقييمات مخاطر ما قبل العمل للمقاولين) - Ergonomic assessments with worker input sections (Ergonomische Beurteilungen) - Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) with multidisciplinary team documentation
Upload documents showing your comprehensive approach to stakeholder participation in risk assessments.\n

Examples of audit or verification documents: - Internal audit reports on risk assessment processes (Informes de Auditoría Interna) - Field verification checklists showing assessment accuracy checks - Redlined or marked-up risk assessments showing field corrections - Risk assessment revision logs with change justifications (Registros de Revisión) - Inspection reports comparing actual conditions to assessed risks (検査報告書) - Document control records showing version history and updates - Field observation cards identifying assessment gaps (بطاقات الملاحظة الميدانية) - Safety committee meeting minutes reviewing assessment effectiveness - Third-party audit findings on worker participation processes - Verification stamps or signatures from qualified safety professionals - Performance metrics on assessment quality and participation rates (Métricas de Desempenho) Detailed Evaluation Criteria: 1\. Audit process evidence: - Formal audit schedule or plan exists - Audit criteria specifically include participation verification - Qualified auditors are identified (internal or external) - Audit frequency is defined and followed 2\. Verification activities: - Field validations compare documented risks to actual conditions - Worker feedback on assessment accuracy is collected - Spot checks or observations verify participation claims - Document reviews check for required signatures/input 3\. Findings and corrections: - Audit findings are documented with clear descriptions - Root cause analysis performed for participation gaps - Corrective actions are assigned, tracked, and closed - Trends in findings are analyzed over time 4\. Continuous improvement: - Updates to assessments based on verification are visible - Policy or procedure revisions reflect lessons learned - Training improvements based on audit findings - Performance indicators show improvement trend - Management review of audit results is documented 5\. Document control: - Version control numbers or dates on assessments - Review/approval signatures or stamps - Distribution records for updated assessments - Obsolete document removal process evidence
Upload evidence showing your risk assessment participation process is audited, verified, and continuously improved.

Examples of acceptable documents: - USA: Stop Work Authority policy, Imminent Danger procedure - Canada: Work Refusal Right documentation, Stop Work procedure - Mexico: Autorización para Detener el Trabajo, Procedimiento de Suspensión - European Union: Arrêt de Travail (France), Arbeitsunterbrechung (Germany), Sospensione del Lavoro (Italy), Paralización del Trabajo (Spain) - Brazil: Parada de Trabalho, Autoridade para Interromper - Argentina: Autoridad para Detener, Procedimiento de Detención - India: परिचालन रोकने का अधिकार (Parichalan Rokne ka Adhikar), Work Stoppage Authority - Japan: 作業中止権 (Sagyō Chūshi-ken), Safety Stop procedure - Saudi Arabia: توقف العمل (Tawaquf al-Amal), Work Cessation Authority - Egypt: سلطة إيقاف العمل (Sultat Iqaf al-Amal) - UAE: صلاحية إيقاف العمل (Salahiyat Iqaf al-Amal) - Turkey: İş Durdurma Yetkisi, Güvenlik Durdurma Prosedürü - Singapore: Stop Work Authority, Work Cessation Rights - Australia: Stop Work Directive, Safety Stand-down Authority Detailed Evaluation Criteria: - Event Date: Clear date(s) when work stoppage occurred (must be within last 24 months) - Initiator Information: Name or job role of person who stopped the work (can be anonymized for privacy) - Reason for Stoppage: Detailed description of the unsafe condition or act that triggered the stop work - Location/Area: Where the work stoppage occurred (department, site, equipment involved) - Affected Operations: What work activities were stopped and number of workers affected - Immediate Actions Taken: Steps taken to secure the area and protect workers - Investigation Findings: Root cause analysis or investigation results of the unsafe condition - Corrective Measures: Actions implemented to address the hazard before work resumed - Restart Authorization: Who approved the resumption of work and when - Time Duration: How long work was stopped (hours, days) - Lessons Learned: Documentation of what was learned and shared with workforce - Recognition/Outcome: Evidence that the person who stopped work was supported (not punished)
Upload documentation demonstrating actual use of stop work authority within the last 24 months.

Examples of acceptable documents: - USA: Stop Work Authority policy, Imminent Danger procedure - Canada: Work Refusal Right documentation, Stop Work procedure - Mexico: Autorización para Detener el Trabajo, Procedimiento de Suspensión - European Union: Arrêt de Travail (France), Arbeitsunterbrechung (Germany), Sospensione del Lavoro (Italy), Paralización del Trabajo (Spain) - Brazil: Parada de Trabalho, Autoridade para Interromper - Argentina: Autoridad para Detener, Procedimiento de Detención - India: परिचालन रोकने का अधिकार (Parichalan Rokne ka Adhikar), Work Stoppage Authority - Japan: 作業中止権 (Sagyō Chūshi-ken), Safety Stop procedure - Saudi Arabia: توقف العمل (Tawaquf al-Amal), Work Cessation Authority - Egypt: سلطة إيقاف العمل (Sultat Iqaf al-Amal) - UAE: صلاحية إيقاف العمل (Salahiyat Iqaf al-Amal) - Turkey: İş Durdurma Yetkisi, Güvenlik Durdurma Prosedürü - Singapore: Stop Work Authority, Work Cessation Rights - Australia: Stop Work Directive, Safety Stand-down Authority Detailed Evaluation Criteria: - Authorization Statement: Document must explicitly state that managers and supervisors have the authority to stop work when unsafe conditions are observed - Scope of Authority: Clear definition of which management levels (supervisors, managers, directors) possess this authority - Triggering Conditions: Specific circumstances that warrant work stoppage (imminent danger, unsafe acts, hazardous conditions, equipment failures) - Protection Clause: Written assurance that personnel exercising stop work authority will not face retaliation, discipline, or negative consequences - Communication Protocol: Process for notifying affected workers and upper management when work is stopped - Documentation Requirements: Forms or procedures for recording the work stoppage event
Upload your organization's stop work authority documentation that shows managers and supervisors have the authority to stop unsafe work.

Examples of acceptable documents: - USA: Stop Work Authority policy, Imminent Danger procedure - Canada: Work Refusal Right documentation, Stop Work procedure - Mexico: Autorización para Detener el Trabajo, Procedimiento de Suspensión - European Union: Arrêt de Travail (France), Arbeitsunterbrechung (Germany), Sospensione del Lavoro (Italy), Paralización del Trabajo (Spain) - Brazil: Parada de Trabalho, Autoridade para Interromper - Argentina: Autoridad para Detener, Procedimiento de Detención - India: परिचालन रोकने का अधिकार (Parichalan Rokne ka Adhikar), Work Stoppage Authority - Japan: 作業中止権 (Sagyō Chūshi-ken), Safety Stop procedure - Saudi Arabia: توقف العمل (Tawaquf al-Amal), Work Cessation Authority - Egypt: سلطة إيقاف العمل (Sultat Iqaf al-Amal) - UAE: صلاحية إيقاف العمل (Salahiyat Iqaf al-Amal) - Turkey: İş Durdurma Yetkisi, Güvenlik Durdurma Prosedürü - Singapore: Stop Work Authority, Work Cessation Rights - Australia: Stop Work Directive, Safety Stand-down Authority Detailed Evaluation Criteria: - Universal Authority Statement: Document must explicitly state that ALL personnel, regardless of position or employment status, can stop work - Inclusion of Worker Types: Clear mention that contractors, subcontractors, temporary workers, and visitors (where applicable) have this authority - Exercise Process: Step-by-step procedure for how any worker initiates a work stoppage - Immediate Actions: What workers should do immediately after stopping work (secure area, notify supervisor, move to safe location) - Notification Chain: Who must be informed and in what timeframe after work is stopped - Investigation Process: How the unsafe condition will be assessed and by whom - Restart Authorization: Clear criteria and approval process for resuming work after stoppage - Good Faith Protection: Statement protecting workers from punishment when stop work authority is exercised in good faith
Upload documentation that demonstrates all workers (including contractors, temporary workers, and visitors where applicable) have stop work authority.

Examples of acceptable documents: - USA: Stop Work Training records, Safety Training Certificates - Canada: COR Training Records, Work Refusal Training Documentation - Mexico: Registros de Capacitación en Suspensión de Trabajo - European Union: Registres de Formation (France), Schulungsnachweis (Germany), Registri di Formazione (Italy), Registros de Formación (Spain) - Brazil: Registros de Treinamento em Parada de Trabalho - Argentina: Registros de Capacitación en Detención de Trabajo - India: प्रशिक्षण अभिलेख (Prashikshan Abhilekh), Safety Training Records - Japan: 研修記録 (Kenshū Kiroku), Safety Training Documentation - Saudi Arabia: سجلات التدريب (Sijillat al-Tadrib) - Egypt: سجلات تدريب السلامة (Sijillat Tadrib al-Salama) - UAE: وثائق التدريب (Wathaiq al-Tadrib) - Turkey: Eğitim Kayıtları, Güvenlik Eğitimi Belgeleri - Singapore: Training Attendance Records, Safety Competency Certificates - Australia: Training Registers, Competency Assessment Records Detailed Evaluation Criteria: - Training Roster/Attendance: Names or employee identification numbers of participants who completed training - Training Dates: When training was conducted (must show ongoing/recent training within reasonable timeframe) - Duration and Format: Length of training session and delivery method (classroom, online, toolbox talk) - Content Coverage: Topics covered including: when to stop work, how to stop work, protection from retaliation, restart procedures - Comprehension Verification: Evidence of knowledge testing, quiz scores, or competency assessments - Trainer Qualifications: Name and credentials of person who conducted the training - New Employee Training: Evidence that new hires receive stop work authority training during onboarding - Refresher Training: Schedule or evidence of periodic retraining for existing employees - Language/Accessibility: Training provided in languages understood by workers and accommodations made as needed
Upload training documentation showing workers have been trained on stop work authority.

Examples include: - New employee safety orientation handbook or manual - Safety orientation presentation slides or training modules - Worker safety induction checklist or sign-off forms - Online safety orientation course content or Learning Management System (LMS) screenshots - Safety orientation video scripts or training guides - New hire safety training schedule or curriculum - Orientation completion certificates or training records template Evaluation Criteria: - Documentation clearly shows a formal, structured orientation program - Content addresses health and safety topics relevant to the workplace - Materials indicate the program applies to workers/employees - Evidence shows the orientation occurs before job assignment begins - No reference to contractor participation requirements
Upload your organization's health and safety orientation materials used for new workers.

Examples include: - Safety orientation policy stating requirements for all site personnel - Contractor safety management procedure or handbook - Site access control procedures requiring safety orientation - Visitor and contractor orientation sign-in sheets or registers - Combined worker and contractor orientation training materials - Safety orientation matrix showing requirements by personnel type - Gate pass or site access procedures linked to orientation completion - Contractor prequalification documents requiring orientation compliance Evaluation Criteria: - Documentation explicitly states orientation applies to both employees and contractors - Clear requirement that all contractors complete orientation before site access - Same or equivalent orientation content provided to workers and contractors - Process for tracking and verifying contractor orientation completion - Evidence of enforcement mechanisms for contractor compliance
Upload your organization's health and safety orientation materials that demonstrate inclusion of both workers and contractors.

Examples include: - Field-based training checklists or Job Safety Analysis (JSA) training records - Hands-on training verification forms or skill demonstration checklists - Mentorship or buddy system program documentation - On-the-job training (OJT) guides or workplace trainer materials - Practical evaluation forms or competency assessment tools - Task-specific qualification records or certification tracking - Equipment operation training logs or authorization cards - Workplace hazard walk-through documentation or area-specific training - Safety observation cards used during practical training phases Evaluation Criteria: - Evidence of classroom/online training for all personnel - Additional practical, hands-on, or field-based training components for workers - Clear distinction between worker and contractor orientation requirements - Competency verification methods beyond knowledge testing (practical demonstrations, observations) - Documentation of extended orientation period with progressive skill development - Contractor orientation limited to classroom or computer-based format
Upload documentation showing your multi-level orientation approach.

Examples include: - Contractor field verification or practical assessment requirements - Site-specific contractor competency evaluation forms - Contractor mentorship or supervised work period documentation - Multi-day contractor orientation schedules with practical components - Contractor skill demonstration requirements or trade-specific assessments - Joint worker-contractor training session materials with hands-on elements - Contractor workplace familiarization tour documentation - Emergency response drill participation records for contractors - Contractor-specific hazard recognition field exercises - Toolbox talk participation requirements during contractor orientation period Evaluation Criteria: - Comprehensive orientation program applies equally to workers and contractors - Both groups receive training beyond classroom/computer-based methods - Evidence of practical, hands-on, or field-based components for contractors - Competency verification for contractors includes practical demonstrations - Documentation shows extended orientation with skill development for all personnel - Clear tracking of both knowledge and practical competency achievement - No distinction in orientation depth between workers and contractors
Upload documentation demonstrating comprehensive orientation for all personnel.

Examples include: - United States: Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) 10-hour or 30-hour cards - Canada: Workplace Hazardous Materials Information System (WHMIS) certificates - Mexico: Certificados de capacitación - European Union countries: Attestations de formation - India: प्रशिक्षण प्रमाणपत्र (Training certificates) - Singapore: Training certificates from Ministry of Manpower - Australia: White cards for construction - Brazil: Certificados de treinamento - Argentina: Certificados de capacitación - Japan: 修了証明書 (Completion certificates) - Kingdom of Saudi Arabia: شهادات التدريب (Training certificates) - Egypt: شهادات التدريب (Training certificates) - Turkey: Eğitim sertifikaları - UAE: شهادات التدريب (Training certificates) Evaluation Criteria: - Documents show evidence of safety training delivery - Records include participant names and dates - Training content covers general safety topics - Documentation demonstrates consistent training activities - Training applies broadly to all workers without role-specific customization
Upload completion certificates or transcripts from safety training courses.

Evaluation Criteria: - Documents demonstrate training customization for specific job roles - Training matrix identifies different requirements for different positions - Evidence shows some (not all) positions have role-specific training - Records link training completion to specific job requirements - Documentation includes hazard-specific or task-specific training elements
Upload a training matrix showing required training by job role for some positions.

Examples include: - Safety meeting presentations showing injury rates - Monthly safety bulletins with accident statistics - Safety dashboard screenshots displaying TRIR trends - Workplace injury reports shared with employees - Safety performance posters showing incident counts - Email communications about recordable injuries - Safety newsletters with lost time statistics - Toolbox talk records discussing injury data - Safety committee meeting minutes showing injury metrics shared with workforce Evaluation Criteria: - Document clearly shows lagging indicator metrics (injuries, incidents, or fatalities that have already occurred) - Evidence of actual communication to workers (not just management reports) - Metrics are workplace safety-related - Communication method reaches entire workforce
Upload documents that show how your organization communicates Total Recordable Incident Rate (TRIR), fatalities, lost time injuries, or days away from work statistics to workers.

Examples include: - Safety dashboards showing both TRIR and safety training completion rates - Monthly reports displaying injuries alongside hazard reporting numbers - Safety presentations combining incident rates with safety observation counts - Performance boards showing lost time injuries and near-miss reporting trends - Safety newsletters featuring both accident statistics and inspection completion percentages - Worker communications showing injuries and participation in safety programs - Safety meeting materials with both incident data and preventive maintenance completion - Email updates combining recordable injuries with safety walk participation rates Evaluation Criteria: - Documents show both lagging indicators (past incidents) and leading indicators (proactive safety activities) - Leading indicators demonstrate preventive actions (training completed, inspections done, observations submitted, hazards identified) - Evidence of systematic communication to entire workforce - Clear distinction between reactive and proactive metrics
Upload documents showing both past incident data (lagging indicators) AND leading indicators are communicated to workers.

Examples include: - Comprehensive safety reports showing TRIR, training rates, and productivity impacts - Dashboard screenshots displaying injuries, inspections, and cost of incidents - Safety presentations combining incident rates, hazard reporting, and operational downtime - Performance communications showing lost time, safety participation, and quality metrics affected by safety - Monthly updates featuring accidents, preventive actions, and customer satisfaction related to safety - Safety bulletins with injuries, observations, and project delays from incidents - Worker communications showing all three metric types including insurance costs or production losses - Town hall presentations linking safety performance to business objectives Evaluation Criteria: - Documents contain all three metric types: lagging, leading, and business impact - Business impact metrics show connection between safety and operational/financial performance (costs, productivity, quality, delivery, reputation) - Communication demonstrates integration of safety with business goals - Evidence reaches entire workforce, not just management
Upload documents showing lagging indicator, leading indicator, AND business-related safety impacts communicated to workers.

Examples include: - Safety communications emphasizing Serious Injury and Fatality (SIF) rates with prevention activities - Reports focusing on life-changing injuries, critical control verifications, and operational impacts - Dashboards highlighting potential SIF events, high-risk activity monitoring, and business consequences - Safety updates featuring actual/potential fatalities, critical risk management actions, and productivity metrics - Communications showing serious incident investigations, preventive barriers, and cost implications - Performance reports emphasizing high-severity incidents, control effectiveness, and project impacts - Safety alerts focusing on life-threatening hazards, critical control performance, and business disruption - Leadership communications prioritizing serious injury prevention with business case data Evaluation Criteria: - Lagging indicators specifically focus on serious injuries, fatalities, or high-potential incidents (not just all recordables) - Leading indicators relate to serious injury and fatality prevention (critical control monitoring, high-risk activity management) - Clear emphasis on preventing life-changing events rather than minor injuries - Business metrics connect serious incident prevention to organizational success - Communication reaches entire workforce with focus on most critical risks
Upload documents showing lagging indicators with serious injury and fatality metrics, leading indicators, AND business impacts communicated to workers.

Examples include: - Safety Trained Supervisor (STS) certification preparation program (United States) - Construction Health and Safety Technician (CHST) study support policy (United States) - Occupational Health and Safety Technician (OHST) certification pathway (United States) - National Examination Board in Occupational Safety and Health (NEBOSH) certification support (United Kingdom and Commonwealth countries) - Canadian Registered Safety Professional (CRSP) development program (Canada) - Técnico em Segurança do Trabalho certification support (Brazil) - Licenciatura en Seguridad e Higiene certification pathway (Argentina) - Safety Officer certification program - Safety Engineer certification support - Occupational Safety Specialist certification pathway - Workplace Safety Officer certification program - Occupational Safety Expert certification support - Environment, Health and Safety Professional certification pathway Evaluation Criteria: - Document clearly outlines available certification options for managers and supervisors - Shows organizational support (financial, time allowance, or resources) - Identifies certification requirements and eligibility criteria - Demonstrates commitment to professional development in safety - Includes tracking or recognition of achieved certifications - Aligns certifications with organizational safety management system requirements

Upload your safety certification pathway document, certification support policy, or professional development program that shows organizational support for manager and supervisor safety certifications.

Examples include: - Training matrix showing Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) required courses - European Union workplace safety directive training requirements list - Canadian Occupational Health and Safety (OHS) training schedule - Australian Work Health and Safety (WHS) training register - Brazilian Regulatory Standard (NR) training matrix - Mexican Official Standard (NOM) training requirements - Japanese Industrial Safety and Health Act (ISHA) training list - Saudi Arabia Occupational Safety and Health (OSH) compliance training document - Singapore Workplace Safety and Health (WSH) training schedule - Indian Factories Act training requirements - Turkish OHS training program - Egyptian labor law safety training matrix - United Arab Emirates (UAE) Environment, Health and Safety (EHS) training requirements - Argentina workplace safety training schedule Evaluation Criteria: - Document clearly identifies managers and supervisors as the target audience - Lists specific regulatory training requirements (course names, regulatory citations, or legal references) - Shows training frequency or renewal periods - Includes completion tracking mechanism or responsible party designation
Upload your organization's training matrix, training program document, or training requirements spreadsheet that lists the legally required health and safety training for managers and supervisors.

Examples include: - Role based competency matrix with safety elements - Position-specific hazard training assessments Evaluation Criteria: - Document demonstrates analysis of specific job roles or departments - Shows differentiation between various management/supervisor positions - Links training requirements to specific workplace hazards or safety responsibilities - Identifies training beyond minimum regulatory requirements - Includes methodology for determining role-specific needs
Upload your training needs analysis or role-specific training assessment that shows customized health and safety training based on manager and supervisor responsibilities
Examples include:\n- Safety leadership workshop presentations\n- Executive safety leadership development curriculum
Upload your health and safety leadership training program materials, curriculum outline, or presentation slides that demonstrate comprehensive leadership development in safety.

Examples include: - Health and Safety Performance Monitoring Policy - Key Performance Indicator (KPI) Tracking Procedure - Safety Metrics and Measurement Program - Performance Evaluation Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) - Health and Safety Dashboard Reports with measurement frequency - Monthly/Quarterly Safety Performance Reports showing regular monitoring - Inspection and Observation Schedule Documents - Leading and Lagging Indicator Tracking Sheets Evaluation Criteria: - Document identifies specific health and safety elements being monitored - Clear measurement methods or metrics are defined - Frequency of monitoring and measurement activities is specified - Performance evaluation processes are described - Responsibilities for monitoring activities are assigned
Upload a document that describes your organization's approach to monitoring and measuring health and safety performance.

Examples include: - Health and Safety Internal Audit Procedure - Safety Management System (SMS) Audit Program - Internal Inspection and Audit Policy - Workplace Safety Audit Protocol - Health and Safety Compliance Audit Guidelines - Annual Audit Schedule and Checklist - Internal Audit Report Template or Completed Audit Reports - Corrective Action Tracking from Internal Audits Evaluation Criteria for Internal Audit Program: - Audit scope covers health and safety management system elements - Audit frequency and schedule are defined - Auditor qualifications or training requirements are specified - Audit methodology and criteria are documented - Process for findings, corrective actions, and follow-up is described - Audit reporting and communication procedures are included
Upload a document that describes your internal audit process for the health and safety management system.

Examples include: - Management Review Procedure or Policy - Annual Health and Safety Review Protocol - Safety Performance Board Review Documents - Health and Safety Planning Documents Evaluation Criteria for Management Review Process: - Minimum annual review frequency is specified - Management review inputs are defined (audit results, incident trends, regulatory changes, etc.) - Senior management participation is required - Review outputs include decisions and actions for improvement - Resource allocation considerations are included - Communication of review outcomes is addressed
Upload a document that describes your management review process for the health and safety management system, conducted at least annually.

Examples include: - Safety Culture Assessment Procedure - Safety Climate Survey Program Documentation - Employee Safety Perception Survey and Analysis Reports - Safety Culture Maturity Assessment Tools - Safety Culture Gap Analysis Reports - Pulse Survey Results on Safety Attitudes - Safety Culture Improvement Action Plans Evaluation Criteria for Safety Culture Evaluation Program: - Method for assessing safety culture or climate is defined - Assessment frequency is specified - Employee participation mechanisms are described - Data collection and analysis procedures are documented - Culture assessment tools or survey instruments are identified - Process for acting on culture assessment findings is included - Links between culture evaluation and performance improvement are established
Upload a document that describes how your organization evaluates its health and safety culture or climate.

Evaluation Criteria: - Document clearly identifies specific lagging indicators (incident rates, injury frequencies, lost time statistics) - Documentation shows integration with annual business planning or objective-setting processes - Metrics are quantifiable and time-bound
Upload a document showing the lagging indicators your organization tracks for health and safety performance.

Examples include: - Corporate balanced scorecard procedures - Key Performance Indicator (KPI) frameworks Evaluation Criteria: - Evidence of tracking at the highest organizational level (board, executive committee, senior leadership team) - Documentation shows integration with annual business planning or objective-setting processes
Upload a policy, procedure, or program document that describes how safety metrics are incorporated into executive or board-level business objectives.

Examples include: - Incident reporting procedure or policy - Accident reporting program - Injury and illness reporting guidelines - Event notification procedure - Safety reporting protocol - Occurrence reporting system documentation - Near-miss reporting procedure Evaluation Criteria: - The document will be evaluated for: - Clear definition of what constitutes a reportable incident - Specified reporting timelines - Identified responsible parties for reporting - Reporting methods or forms referenced - Categories of incidents covered (injuries, illnesses, near-misses, property damage)
Upload your organization's procedure, policy, or program that explains how work-related incidents are reported.

Examples include: - Completed incident report forms - Injury and illness logs (such as OSHA 300 logs in the US, Form 7 in Canada) - Accident registers or databases Evaluation Criteria: - The document will be evaluated for: - Actual incident entries with dates and basic details - Consistent record-keeping format - Range of incident types documented - Regular entries showing ongoing documentation - Compliance with recordkeeping requirements
Upload documentation showing actual recorded work-related incidents.

Examples include: - Incident investigation procedure or policy - Accident investigation program - Post-incident response procedure Evaluation Criteria: - The document will be evaluated for: - Defined investigation triggers and thresholds - Investigation team composition and qualifications - Timeline requirements for starting and completing investigations - Investigation methodology or techniques described - Evidence collection and witness interview processes - Documentation and reporting requirements
Upload your organization's procedure, policy, or program that describes how incident investigations are conducted.

Examples include: - Completed root cause analysis reports - Investigation reports with Five Whys analysis, Fishbone diagrams, or Fault Tree Analysis - Corrective and Preventive Action (CAPA) plans - Completed investigation forms with root cause sections and action items Evaluation Criteria: - The document will be evaluated for: - Systematic root cause analysis methodology applied - Multiple contributing factors identified beyond immediate causes - Clear corrective actions linked to identified root causes - Preventive actions to avoid recurrence - Responsible parties and completion dates assigned - Evidence of follow-up or closure verification - Communication of findings and lessons learned
Upload completed analyses showing root cause determination and resulting corrective and preventive actions.

Examples of acceptable documents: - Near Miss Reporting Procedure or Policy - Close Call Reporting Process Document - Incident Reporting Procedure that includes near miss reporting - Safety Management System (SMS) manual section on near miss reporting Evaluation Criteria: - Document clearly defines what constitutes a near miss - Specifies who should report near misses - Describes the reporting method (form, system, verbal, etc.) - Identifies to whom near misses should be reported - Outlines timeframes for reporting
Upload your organization's procedure that explains how employees report near misses.

Examples of acceptable documents: - Completed near miss report forms - Near miss log or register (may redact sensitive information) - Close call tracking spreadsheet - Safety database reports showing near miss entries - Monthly or quarterly near miss summary reports Evaluation Criteria: - Shows actual near misses have been recorded - Includes date, location, and description of near miss events - Demonstrates consistent documentation practice - Contains multiple entries showing ongoing reporting
Upload examples of completed near miss reports or logs showing that near misses are being documented in your organization.

Examples of acceptable documents: - Near Miss Investigation Procedure or Policy - Incident Investigation Program that includes near miss investigations - Investigation methodology documentation Evaluation Criteria: - Outlines investigation process steps - Identifies who conducts investigations - Specifies investigation timelines - Includes method for gathering information - Shows systematic approach to determining what happened
Upload your investigation procedure, policy, program, or investigation form specifically for near misses.

Examples of acceptable documents: - Completed near miss investigation reports with root cause analysis - Corrective and Preventive Action (CAPA) forms from near miss investigations - 5-Why Analysis worksheets with resulting actions - Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagrams with corrective measures - Fault tree analysis with preventive actions Evaluation Criteria: - Demonstrates systematic root cause analysis methodology - Identifies underlying causes beyond immediate causes - Lists specific corrective actions to address immediate hazards - Details preventive actions to avoid recurrence - Assigns responsibility for implementing actions - Includes timelines for completion of actions - Shows linkage between identified root causes and chosen actions
Upload completed investigation reports showing root cause analysis and the corrective and preventive actions (CAPA) taken.

Examples include: - Root Cause Analysis Procedure or Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) - Incident Investigation Program that contains RCA methodology section - Health and Safety Management System Manual with RCA process chapter - Written RCA protocols or guidelines document Evaluation Criteria: - The document must clearly identify and describe a structured root cause analysis methodology or process. - It should specify when RCA is required, who performs it, and the general steps or techniques to be used (such as 5 Whys, Fishbone Diagram, Fault Tree Analysis, or other systematic approaches)
Upload your organization's written documentation that describes your root cause analysis process for health and safety incidents and near misses.

Examples include: - Filled-out RCA worksheets or forms - Incident investigation reports with documented root cause analysis sections - Completed 5 Whys analysis sheets - Documented Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagrams from actual incidents - Fault Tree Analysis reports Evaluation Criteria: - The completed forms or reports must demonstrate actual use of the structured RCA process identified in the policy/procedure. - Documents should show systematic analysis of real incidents or near misses, identification of root causes (not just immediate causes), and be consistently formatted according to the organization's RCA methodology.
Upload examples of completed root cause analysis documentation.

Examples include: - RCA methodology training certificates (internal or external courses) - Training attendance sheets for RCA workshops or seminars - Professional certification in incident investigation or RCA techniques - Training matrices showing completed RCA training for HSE personnel - Course completion records from online or in-person RCA training - Learning management system (LMS) transcripts showing RCA course completion Evaluation Criteria: - Training evidence must specifically relate to root cause analysis methodology and be linked to HSE staff or investigation team members. - Documents should show formal training completion, include dates, and identify the RCA methodology or techniques covered (matching those in the organization's procedures).
Upload evidence of root cause analysis training for HSE staff or dedicated investigation team members.

Examples include: - RCA methodology training certificates (internal or external courses) - Training attendance sheets for RCA workshops or seminars - Professional certification in incident investigation or RCA techniques - Training matrices showing completed RCA training for operational management and/or frontline workers - Course completion records from online or in-person RCA training - Learning management system (LMS) transcripts showing RCA course completion Evaluation Criteria: - Training evidence must demonstrate that operational management (supervisors, managers, team leads) and/or workers (frontline employees, operators, technicians) have received formal instruction in root cause analysis. - Documents should show formal training completion, include dates, and identify the RCA methodology or techniques covered (matching those in the organization's procedures).
Upload evidence of root cause analysis training for operational management and/or frontline workers.

Examples include: - CAPA procedure or standard operating procedure - Incident management program with CAPA requirements - Safety management system manual with CAPA section - Continuous improvement procedure - Non-conformance management procedure - Action tracking policy Evaluation Criteria: - Document demonstrates organizational requirements for developing corrective and preventative actions - Register/log shows actual corrective and preventative actions have been identified - Evidence indicates some level of CAPA implementation, even if inconsistent
Upload documentation that shows your organization has established requirements for creating corrective and preventative actions.

Examples include: - CAPA tracking spreadsheet with closure dates - Action item register showing completion percentages Evaluation Criteria: - Document establishes clear requirements for CAPA development and closure timelines - Register/log demonstrates consistent tracking of actions from identification to closure - Evidence shows actions are completed within defined timeframes - Documentation indicates systematic approach to CAPA management
Upload a register, log, or tracking system showing corrective and preventative actions with completion status.
Examples include:\n- Audit reports verifying corrective action implementation\n- Post-implementation review documentation
Upload records demonstrating verification of corrective action effectiveness.

Examples of acceptable documents include: - Corrective and preventative action procedure specifying worker involvement in developing and reviewing actions - Safety management system procedure requiring worker input when determining action effectiveness - Continuous improvement policy mandating worker participation in action planning and verification - Standard operating procedure for collaborative corrective and preventative action development sessions Evaluation Criteria: - CAPA development role: Explicit requirement for workers to help develop corrective and preventative actions - Effectiveness evaluation: Workers participate in determining whether actions resolve root causes - Verification process: Documented method for workers to confirm action implementation - Feedback mechanism: Process for workers to report if actions are not effective - Closure requirements: Worker input required before investigation closure - Continuous improvement link: Connection between worker feedback and action refinement
Upload a program, policy, or procedure that requires workers to participate in developing corrective and preventative actions and evaluating their effectiveness.

Examples of acceptable documents include: - Safety bulletins, alerts, or newsletters summarizing incidents and corrective actions - Toolbox talk records or safety moment presentations discussing investigation findings - Posted investigation summaries or lesson-learned notices - Email communications or intranet announcements sharing root cause findings and corrective and preventative actions - Meeting minutes from safety committee meetings where investigation results were shared - Digital communication logs from safety apps or platforms Evaluation Criteria: - Audience identification: Communication reaches workers and relevant interested parties - Content completeness: Includes root cause analysis findings and corrective and preventative actions - Incident coverage: Addresses both incidents and near misses - Timeliness: Evidence of regular or event-triggered communications. Accessibility Communication method is accessible to all affected workers
Upload documents showing how your organization shares the results of incident and near miss investigations with workers and other interested parties.

Examples of acceptable documents include: - Incident investigation procedure specifying worker participation requirements - Health and safety policy mandating employee involvement in investigation teams - Standard operating procedure for forming investigation teams that include frontline workers - Safety management manual sections describing worker roles in investigations - Documented process for selecting and training worker investigation team members - Joint health and safety committee terms of reference including investigation responsibilities Evaluation Criteria: - Mandatory language: Clear requirement (not optional) for worker involvement - Scope of involvement: Applies to both incident and near miss investigations - Role definition: Describes specific worker responsibilities in the investigation process - Selection process: Explains how workers are chosen for investigation participation - Documentation requirements: Specifies how worker involvement is recorded
Upload a program, policy, or procedure that requires workers to participate in incident and near miss investigations.

Examples of acceptable documents include: - Root cause analysis procedure requiring worker input and participation - Investigation methodology document specifying worker involvement in cause identification - Safety management system manual describing worker roles in determining underlying causes Evaluation Criteria: - Root cause analysis requirements: Explicit reference to worker participation in identifying root causes - Methodology reference: Names specific analysis techniques workers use or support - Training provisions: Evidence that workers receive training on root cause analysis methods - Documentation of contributions: Process for recording worker input during analysis - Integration with investigation: Clear connection between worker involvement and formal investigation steps
Upload a program, policy, or procedure that requires workers to participate in identifying root causes during incident investigations.

Evaluation Criteria: - Document includes both lagging indicators (incident rates, injuries) and leading indicators (training completion, audit scores, hazard reports) - Documentation shows integration with annual business planning or objective-setting processes - Metrics are quantifiable and time-bound
Upload a document showing both lagging and leading indicators your organization tracks for health and safety performance.

Examples include: - Corporate balanced scorecard procedures - Key Performance Indicator (KPI) frameworks Evaluation Criteria: - Evidence of tracking at the highest organizational level (board, executive committee, senior leadership team) - Documentation shows integration with annual business planning or objective-setting processes - Metrics are quantifiable and time-bound
Upload a policy, procedure, or program document that describes how safety metrics are incorporated into executive or board-level business objectives.

Evaluation Criteria: - Balanced scorecard includes all three metric categories: lagging, leading, and business impact - Documentation shows integration with annual business planning or objective-setting processes - Metrics are quantifiable and time-bound
Upload a document including lagging indicators, leading indicators, and business impact metrics your organization tracks for health and safety performance.

Evaluation Criteria: - Scorecard specifically addresses SIF potential through metrics such as: SIF actual events, SIF potential incidents, STKY near-misses, high-energy exposure tracking, or critical control verification - Documentation shows integration with annual business planning or objective-setting processes - Metrics are quantifiable and time-bound
Upload a document including lagging indicators, leading indicators, and business impact metrics your organization tracks for health and safety performance.
Compliance is the floor, not the ceiling. This question evaluates how systematically your organization identifies, monitors, and integrates the rules that govern your operations.\n

Select the answer that most closely aligns to the organization's practice regarding health and safety legal requirements and other standards that apply to work (consensus standards or contractual requirements for health and safety).
A. The organization has not clearly identified what sets of health and safety legal requirements it is to follow.

B. The organization evaluates health and safety legal requirements that apply when something goes wrong. Most commonly after an incident or regulatory enforcement action. There may be some written policies and procedures for health and safety.
C. The organization has a documented process for identifying the health and safety legal requirements that apply to the organization.

D. The organization has a documented process for identifying the health and safety legal and other requirements that apply to the organization. There are written health and safety procedures that correspond to the legal and other requirements. The register or collection of legal and other requirements is periodically reviewed and updated.

E. The organization has a documented process for identifying the health and safety legal and other requirements that apply to the organization. There are written health and safety procedures that correspond to the legal and other requirements. The register or collection of legal and other requirements is periodically reviewed and updated with worker support and workers are engaged in setting the requirements that govern their health and safety at work.

Why it is needed: This acts as the "Master List." It proves the organization has a deliberate process for scanning the legal landscape. This must include "Other" non-regulatory standards, showing that you are holding yourselves to a higher professional bar than just the legal minimum.

Upload a document that describes how your organization identifies both legal requirements AND other requirements that apply to your work. Other requirements include voluntary standards you follow, customer contract requirements, industry codes of practice, or internal corporate requirements. A spreadsheet, database, or document listing the specific regulations and standards applicable to your site (e.g., "29 CFR 1910.147," "NFPA 70E," or "Contractual Safety Exhibit A").

Upload one or more safety procedures that your organization created to meet specific legal OR other requirements. Include at least one procedure that corresponds to a voluntary standard, contractual requirement, or industry code of practice.
Why it is needed: This acts as the "Master List." It proves the organization has a deliberate process for scanning the legal landscape.

Upload a document that describes how your organization finds and tracks the health and safety laws that apply to your work. This document should explain the steps your organization takes to identify which laws and regulations you must follow. A spreadsheet, database, or document listing the specific regulations and standards applicable to your site (e.g., "29 CFR 1910.147").
